focus: Connected speech basics — linking, reduction, and natural flow
focus_ar: الكلام المتصل — الربط والاختصار والتدفق الطبيعي
arabic_challenge: Arabic speakers tend to pronounce each word as a separate unit. In natural English, words BLEND into each other. Mastering this is what makes you sound 'American' vs 'robotic'.

rule_1.name: Consonant-to-Vowel Linking
rule_1.description: When a word ends in a consonant and the next word starts with a vowel, they link together.

rule_2.name: Vowel-to-Vowel Linking (glide insertion)
rule_2.description: When a word ends in a vowel and the next starts with a vowel, insert /w/ or /j/ to bridge them.

rule_3.name: Consonant Reduction
rule_3.description: When the same consonant ends one word and starts the next, say it only ONCE.

rule_4.name: Function Word Reduction
rule_4.description: Common small words (to, for, and, of, a, the) reduce in fast speech.

daily_drills.slow_then_fast: Say each phrase SLOWLY first (word by word). Then say it again FAST (linked). Record both versions.
sentences: Can I turn it off now?
Pick up an apple and put it on the table.
I want to think about it — not at all sure yet.

daily_drills.task: Practice inserting the invisible /w/ or /j/ between vowels. Record 5 sentences using these phrases.

daily_drills.focus: Rule 3 — Consonant Reduction + Rule 4 — Function Words
daily_drills.task: Practice saying the REDUCED forms naturally. These are not 'wrong' — they are how Americans actually speak.

dialogues.a: Hey, do you wanna go out?
dialogues.b: I dunno... I gotta lotta work.

dialogues.a: Can you pick it up for me?
dialogues.b: Not at all! I'm gonna do it now.

daily_drills.task: Practice both the slow and linked versions of each dialogue. Record the LINKED version.

daily_drills.paragraph_slow: I want to go out and pick up an apple. I am going to eat it. Then I have to go to work. I am kind of tired but it is not a problem. I think about it all the time.
daily_drills.paragraph_linked: I wanna go-wout 'n pi-kup a-napple. I'm gonna eat-it. Then I hafta go tuh work. I'm kinda tired bu-dit's no-da problem. I thin-kabou-dit all the time.
daily_drills.task: Read the slow version first. Then practice the linked version until it flows naturally. Record the linked version.

daily_drills.challenge: Record yourself telling a 60-second story about your day. Focus on LINKING words together — never pause between every word. Use reduced forms (gonna, wanna, gotta) where natural.
tips: Don't stop between words — let them flow into each other
Unstressed syllables should be FAST and quiet (schwa!)
Stressed words should be LOUD and clear
Reduced forms are correct and natural — use them!

final_assessment.passage: I'm gonna tell you about my day. I woke up early and went outside. The air was kinda cold but I didn't mind. I walked to a nice café near my apartment and picked up a coffee. I hafta say, it was really good. Then I went to work. I wanted to finish everything early so I could go out in the evening. After work I called my friend. I said 'Hey, do you wanna go out?' She said 'I dunno, I gotta lotta things to do.' But she came anyway. We had a great time.
final_assessment.task: Record this passage with full connected speech. This is your FINAL accent assessment for Level 0. Compare to your Week 1 Day 7 recording. The difference should be dramatic.

scoring.90_100: Natural flow. Linking automatic. Reductions smooth. Sounds conversational.
scoring.70_89: Good linking. Occasional pauses between words. Reductions mostly natural.
scoring.50_69: Some linking but still word-by-word in places. Reductions feel forced.
scoring.below_50: Still reading word-by-word. Review weeks 1-5 phonemes then re-attempt connected speech.

level_0_accent_completion_note: This is the FINAL week of Level 0 accent training. After 8 weeks, you should be able to: produce all 44 phonemes (Weeks 1-5), stress words correctly (Week 7), and link words in connected speech (Week 8). The Level 0 Exit Exam (§9.3) will test these skills. If you pass, you advance to Level 1 where accent training continues with Flap T, Glottal Stop, and advanced linking patterns.
